speaker: interviewer
text: It's Wednesday, January 18, 1995, and this is an interview with survivor Margaret Smaye. The interviewer is Sandi Pelly. And we're conducting the interview in Toronto, Canada, in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Margaret Smaye. My maiden name is Mancie Drummer. And my Hebrew name is [HEBREW]. I was born in Sighet, Maramures, Romania. But my parents didn't live there. We moved to a city in Judetal Salaj. In Romanische that's what it was called, the area. And the city's name is Carei Mare in Romanische. And I lived there till about-- till I was about 8 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My immediate family? My-- I had the-- we were five children, three girls and two boys. I was the oldest of the family. And my sister is the next one, Ella, who was with me right through concentration camp. She's alive, and all the others were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The one who's alive is Ella. The next one was Michael, Jachil [INAUDIBLE] in Hebrew. And my little sister was Esther Chaia. And I had a little brother. His name is Sloma Yoseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember the most about growing up with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- what I remember, we were a very religious family in those days. And we had just a typical-- nothing really extraordinary. We-- me being the oldest, I was always expected to help. And I did, I think. And that's about what all I can say about the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drummer, D-R-U-M-M-E-R. And my mother's name was Regina Taback. And my grandparents lived in Sighit, where I was born. And therefore, many summers I used to spend there with my grandparents, aunts, and uncles. My mother was-- her family were sisters and brothers, nine of them. My father's family were 10 in the family. So it was big families. And we used to get together for simchas, for-- and it was very nice for holidays. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just being there and being with my-- the whole family, with my grandparents and aunts and uncles. And it was a lot of fun, a lot of fun. Therefore, it's nice memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a lumber dealer. He used to buy pieces of forests and have them cut out and made into lumber. And this is what he was dealing with. And that is the reason why we moved from Carei Mare to Supuru de Jos, where he was closer to the forests and places. It was a small town, but we had some cousins living there who were partners with my father at the time. And we lived there until we were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about being a child and going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to public school. And I remember I started kindergarten at age four. And I went to a Romanische kindergarten at age four. And at age 5, I was in a German kindergarten, not because the Germans had occupied, but that was the idea. And then at the next, I went to another Romanische kindergarten. And school started there at age seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1928, January 15, in Sighit, Sighit, Maramures. But soon after, my parents moved from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the last-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my grandmother when she passed away, I happened to be there. And that was in Sighit when I was out of school. And after that, I remember a lot of stories about antisemitism, that Jews were-- especially ones with beards, were thrown off trains and a lot of that. But being a child, I didn't pay much attention to that. And the place where I lived became Hungary in 1942. The Hungarian Army marched in. And they re-occupied the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember having personal experiences of antisemitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: In schools, we were always called dirty Jews. And that's about it, really, because the neighborhood I lived, it wasn't-- it wasn't like that, like not much. But mostly at school, like if there was something the kids didn't like, this is what they would call us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the neighborhood you grew up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of my growing up was in Supuru de Jos. And it was a small town. We lived near the railway station and-- with my cousins living there and some of my aunts. My father's sister, I remember her getting married from our house, which was very nice, and after that, visiting with her when she moved away. And just typical, nothing extraordinary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That town must have been about 5,000 population.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, we were about 50 families of Jews. There was one synagogue. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Most of the people I remember were Orthodox, some to larger degrees, some a little less. They were Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said your parents were religious, what did you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Orthodox religious. My mother wore a wig. And my father had a beard until 1942, when he was called in to the Hungarian army, which was not the regular army. It was like a working army. The Jews were not allowed to be in the regular army. And they cut his beard off. And I remember the first time when he came on leave without a beard, we were crying, because, you know, it looked so unusual. We could hardly recognize him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work. She was with us constantly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change when Hitler came to power in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that part. All I remember-- my first recollection is that people from Poland in 1939, '40, like Jewish people from Poland, they were-- they ran from Poland, and they come-- came to Romania at that time. And they were telling us stories, that they are being taken to ghettos and to camps. But as a child, I, I didn't pay much attention. And I really don't know whether my parents really believed all the stories. It was so unbelievable, which we later found out was very much true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents talk about? What do you remember them talking about after the Germans invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that-- at times, my parents were talking that we will go to-- after the Hungarians came in, that we will go to Romania too, because we knew that in Romania, the Jews had it a little better at that time, and they were not going to be sent away. Because they were already talk about gathering up the Jews and taking them to ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between the time the war broke out in Poland and the time that you were living in Hungary, how did your life change in those couple of years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as Jews, we had to be much more cautious where we went and how we went. And like I said, my father was already afraid to travel too much. So at times, he would send me to different places for contracts or money collecting or things like that, because it was much safer for me, although I was still very young, to go than for a man, a Jew, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How else was your life affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just with the talk and with the worries. But in my family, you know, my, my parents tried very much to shelter us, not to have to worry and not to know. They were very, very good at that. Like, you know, they wouldn't discuss things in front of us much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other restrictions put on Jews after 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I am aware of in, in our places. There were not much that I could think of, that any restrictions were-- of course, I'm sure that there were, but I couldn't-- and being a small town made a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first feel that you were in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until the 1943, '44. That's when we started really-- then we felt it's close to home, and the stories are true, because more and more people were coming, trying to come in from Poland and telling us the stories. And that's when we started to prepare. As a matter of fact, we prepared some foods-- which there, of, course were not like now. You had to prepare yourself, to take along for the journey, which never came about. And until-- I remember for Passover, 1944, there were hard to get matzohs, so we had to bake our own. And I remember helping there to bake the matzohs. And it was very sad, that Pesach, because we were afraid that it wouldn't be long until something will be happening. And as a matter of fact, it did soon after.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the stories that the Polish people told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the camps, about the killings, about the ghettos, about the murderings. And some of them were saying that some were hiding and some were partisans and fighting and-- but like I said, most of that we didn't believe. We couldn't accept it, that this could happen. So we just put it out of our minds and went on. My father went on with his life and had to provide, make a living. So-- and when he was in the army, it was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I know that he was called in  and he wasn't far-- stationed far away, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would hear from him. It was close by. I can't remember the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? In, in about 1943. It must have been 1943 when he went to the army. And some of his brothers were also, but they were further away. But him being at the age he was, he wasn't really sent far away, because by that time, my father was in his late 30s. And he wasn't sent too far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very hard on us. It was mostly hard on my mother, having to do everything herself and look after the family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when he came back, he couldn't start again what-- the way he did, because there were restrictions, like the people he dealt with, some of them weren't sure whether he would be able to deliver because, you know, of all the talk and things, so things started to get a little bit bad and harder. And it wasn't easy to, to make a living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back-- he wasn't long gone. He was about six months gone, and then he came back. And he picked up from where he left off, but much, much smaller, in a smaller way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he conceal the fact that he was Jewish when he went into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because he was called in because he was Jewish. Like, you know, he wasn't in the kind of army that they would give them ammunition. He was in a working army because being a Jew. No, he couldn't-- you couldn't conceal that, being Jewish. And he-- he-- we took whatever was dished out. We didn't ask questions. And you have to, because the authorities there were very strict, and sometimes very corrupt, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did things really start to change for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really not until 1944, spring 1944, right after Passover. And all of a sudden, we were-- the gendarmes came to the home without anything, without any notice, without anything knowing. And they said in two hours, you'll be taken from your homes, so pack whatever you can and take along food what you have in the house. And they were standing at the door, and we couldn't leave anymore. We couldn't leave the house. In about two hours, people were bringing-- I mean, the neighbors saw, the non-Jewish neighbors, they tried to bring bread, loaves of bread, because they knew that we would take them. And we were taken by horse and buggy, really, to a ghetto about 30 or 40 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the name of the ghetto was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was in Simleu, Simleu. And it used to be a brick factory, that place. And we were taken there. It was a large, huge, empty space. We had to put up tents for ourself with whatever, made out of sheets or whatever we took along.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and my brothers and sisters. And some of my cousins who lived there also were taken there. And we-- we were given some food, but there was a lot of rain. It was spring and we used to get wet. And the sun in the morning used to dry us out. And then at night, we'd go to sleep. It was cold, damp, hungry, whatever. But as children, we just took it. We thought, oh, let's just change. And we'll-- we were playing and not thinking. Although I was a teenager already, but my sisters and brothers, we were-- we just thought, OK, this will pass. We'll get back home. On the way, as we were going, you know, it was like a whole caravan of cars and, and, and-- it was all with horses and buggies. Some people were coming out to watch. And some people who my father used to deal with were trying to talk to him so he could stay behind, and they would hide him. And somehow they communicated that to him, but he had not-- he didn't want to hear of it. Because they said, the family, we can't take the whole family, just you. And he didn't want to stay behind, because he could have. They would have had him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the journey take to get to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take long. It take-- I don't really remember whether we slept over or it was just the one day. But I think we must have slept over. We must have slept over in the wagons. But somehow I don't remember exactly. But I know when we got there, we were very busy putting up the shelters out of sheets what we had taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were approximately-- four to five weeks, we were in the ghetto. And we already-- Shavu'ot we were celebrating in the ghetto. And during that time already, or the-- after about four weeks people were starting to leave. I mean, they were taking them away. We never knew where. All we just knew that people are being transported out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what people talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I don't really remember exactly. But we all thought people who leave, that's not a good sign, you know? We were better off to stay. So we were happy that we were not the ones who are selected to leave at that time. Until it was our turn in the beginning of June, the end of May or the beginning of June. We were all put on-- into cattle cars, into trains, and we were shipped out. And we were about 50, 60 to a car, like the-- one of the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you leave from? Do you remember what station it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Simleu that we left-- from where the ghetto was, because I think the trains were coming into that area-- there were tracks because of the brick factory there. So they had tracks for trains to come right into the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you go onto the train with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents and some of the other people. We were all-- but really very tightly squeezed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with all your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, and my parents and some cousins. The trains were locked, but there was about a 10-inch opening by the door that they left open for air. That's all we had. And we traveled for about three, two to three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about the train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: That in different station, we would stop sometimes for hours. And some people were nice people. They would come out and try to give us food. And I remember my little brother was so cute. He wasn't even three years old yet. He was standing in that little opening by the door, and people used to give food to him. Sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBS] of course, we, we used to, you know, share it with all the people. And there were pails for washrooms in the corner. It was very degrading. But somehow as long as we were together, we, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we managed, somehow, to pass that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you talked about with your family? What were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember. All I remember, just what  I saw, you know? You know, how my mother and father tried to make us as comfortable as could be, which wasn't very much. At home, like I said, we were really-- and there it was wagon, pushed in. And it was really awful when we had to use the washroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were about 50 or 60 people in each car. People to a-- in each car. Do you remember how many cars there would have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember that. But it was a real long train. And like I said, that train was-- didn't have the priority. Therefore, every time we arrived to a station, they would just let us sit there, sometimes for hours, before the other trains would pass by, especially military trains, you know. Because we could see a lot of German soldiers in other trains going by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever open the doors when they stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The doors stayed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never got off the train in those two or three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never got off the train, no. Never got off the train. But I could remember, I think they were taking off some of the pails from the-- from the wash-- yeah. And we were given new, like, fresh ones. So they would just open a little more there and take them out, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we arrived to Auschwitz, which we didn't know-- we were, at least I was in my family-- if my parents knew, they didn't say it where we are going or what is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when the doors opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the doors opened-- we arrived at night. It was dark. It must have been about midnight. All I could see when the doors opened, a lot of fires, like the fires used to-- like so high. To me it looked like all the way up to the sky, the red-- the red flames.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the smell. Didn't know. Didn't know. Like I said, we were very naive. And the, the doors were opened and there were these men with striped uniforms and saying, get out, get out, get out. Everybody get out. I remember we still had a piece of bread which was left over. So I stuck it in my pocket to give for my little brother. And as we got out of the cars, there again these striped men were saying men to go separately to this side, and women and children to this side. And my father and my little brother, like the older of the young brothers, who was about 11 at the time--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jachil [INAUDIBLE], Michael. Yeah. He-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he went with my father. And they were supposed to stand three to a row. I don't know whoever was with them, because I didn't see them anymore. That was it. They went. And then my mother-- and she was holding my little brother, who was not quite three, my little sister, that's Esther Chaia. And they were three. And then my sister Ella and I and somebody else was standing beside us. And then we were standing there for a little while. And then they asked us to-- then we had to walk closer. Where we were closer, we saw these people sitting there in uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to go back. What was it like when the men were separated from the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't think of it as a big deal. We thought that that's just temporary. We didn't-- now like I said, we were very naive. We didn't think of it that this is permanent, and this is forever. And therefore, we didn't-- we didn't make a big thing of it. All I remember, with my cousin who was-- had also a little boy. He was about five. She also was in the same train with us. And when she got out, one of these-- and her parents were there with her. But her husband wasn't there, because he was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of these uniformed men grabbed the child from her and gave it to her mother. And she says, that's my child. So he said to her, do you want your mother to go to work? Let her have the child so you could go to work. I'm going to stop you there, because we have to change the tape. And then we'll continue.
